# **Comprehensive Survey of Advanced Methodologies for Vehicle Routing Problems with Profits: Exact Solvers, Heuristics, and Learning-Based Optimization**

## **Introduction to the Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits**

The classical Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) and its foundational predecessor, the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), operate under a strict, unyielding assumption: all nodes representing customer demand within a defined network must be visited exactly once.1 First introduced as the truck dispatching problem by Dantzig and Ramser in 1959, the traditional objective in these deterministic settings is universally the minimization of a cost function, typically representing total travel distance, travel time, fuel consumption, or the number of vehicles deployed.2 However, the landscape of modern logistics operations—characterized by the explosion of e-commerce, reverse logistics, dynamic ride-sharing, humanitarian search and rescue operations, and autonomous vehicle path planning—introduces complex scenarios where servicing the entirety of customer demand is either physically impossible due to strict capacity constraints or economically suboptimal.5 This operational reality necessitates a paradigm shift from pure cost minimization to profit maximization, giving rise to the family of routing architectures known as the Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits (VRPP).

In the VRPP paradigm, a profit, prize, or score is associated with each customer node in the graph. The optimization algorithm is therefore tasked with solving two simultaneous, highly coupled, and strongly ![][image1]\-hard combinatorial problems: a knapsack-like selection problem to determine the optimal subset of customers to visit, and a routing problem to determine the optimal sequence in which to visit the selected customers.5 The computational complexity of this dual decision-making process is staggeringly vast. For a network graph containing ![][image2] potential customers (excluding the depot), the total number of possible customer subsets corresponds to ![][image3].9 For any selected subset of ![][image4] customers, evaluating the optimal sequence requires exploring ![][image5] different permutation routes.9 Consequently, the total search space encompassing all possible solutions for a VRPP instance is defined mathematically as ![][image6].9

To grasp the magnitude of this complexity, consider a seemingly modest problem instance of just 50 customers. This formulation yields approximately ![][image7] possible valid sequences.9 Even a highly reduced instance of 20 customers generates over ![][image8] possible solutions.9 If a modern computational processor evaluates one billion solutions per second, resolving the 20-customer instance via exhaustive enumeration would require over 210 years of continuous computation.9 This fundamental mathematical intractability dictates that exact commercial solvers cannot scale efficiently, thereby driving the continuous development of sophisticated exact bounds, hybrid matheuristics, hyper-heuristics, and the rapidly expanding domain of Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO).

This report delivers an exhaustive, mathematically rigorous survey of the state-of-the-art methodologies designed to solve the VRPP and its primary variants. The analysis encompasses exact solvers built upon polyhedral combinatorics and decomposition techniques, matheuristics that blend linear programming with local search, metaheuristics and hyper-heuristics, and deep learning models. Furthermore, a critical examination of non-neural Machine Learning (ML) and non-neural Tabular Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches is provided. These are systematically juxtaposed against computational benchmarks to reveal theoretical limits, execution speeds, and practical optimality gaps in modern Operations Research.

## **Mathematical Formulations and Taxonomy of VRPP Variants**

The literature classifies the VRPP into three dominant variants, differentiated primarily by their treatment of the objective functions and resource constraints.2 Let a complete, undirected graph be defined as ![][image9], where ![][image10] is the set of vertices.14 Vertex ![][image11] represents the starting depot, and vertex ![][image12] represents the ending depot.14 The set ![][image13] represents the edges connecting the nodes.14 A nonnegative profit ![][image14] is associated with each customer node ![][image15], and a symmetric routing cost ![][image16] or travel time ![][image17] is associated with traversing the edge ![][image18].14

Let ![][image19] be a binary decision variable that takes the value of ![][image20] if node ![][image21] is visited, and ![][image11] otherwise. Let ![][image22] be a binary decision variable that equals ![][image20] if the edge ![][image18] is traversed in the final route configuration.

### **The Profitable Tour Problem (PTP)**

The Profitable Tour Problem (PTP) is the most generalized variant in terms of constraint relaxation. It does not explicitly constrain the maximum travel cost, nor does it enforce a minimum threshold on the amount of profit that must be collected.2 Instead, the PTP seeks to maximize a single unconstrained net objective function, explicitly defined as the difference between the total profit collected from the visited nodes and the total routing cost incurred by traversing the chosen edges.2

The mathematical formulation for the PTP seeks to maximize the following objective function:

![][image23]
In this formulation, ![][image24] is a scaling parameter, almost universally set to ![][image20] in standard literature.10 The constraints for the PTP strictly enforce standard degree requirements, ensuring that if a node is visited (![][image25]), exactly one active edge enters the node and exactly one active edge leaves it.10 Furthermore, Generalized Subtour Elimination Constraints (GSECs) are required to ensure that all visited nodes remain continuously connected to the depot, preventing the formation of isolated, disjointed cycles within the graph.10 Multi-vehicle extensions of this model, termed the Capacitated Profitable Tour Problem (CPTP), introduce vehicle capacity limitations and demand fulfillment parameters, effectively transforming the PTP into a highly constrained set-partitioning challenge where split deliveries may be optimized to increase net profitability.15

### **The Prize-Collecting Travelling Salesman Problem (PCTSP)**

The Prize-Collecting Travelling Salesman Problem (PCTSP), fundamentally introduced by E. Balas for the complex scheduling of rolling mills in the steel industry, alters the structural optimization goal.2 In the PCTSP, the primary objective reverts to the classical TSP goal of minimizing the total routing cost.2 However, the problem introduces a strict, hard constraint dictating that the sum of the profits collected from the visited nodes must meet or exceed a predefined minimum threshold, mathematically denoted as ![][image26] or the quota.2

The objective function is defined strictly as:

![][image27]
This is subject to the strict quota constraint:

![][image28]
The PCTSP is deeply connected to other routing paradigms from a theoretical standpoint. When the quota ![][image26] is set precisely to zero, the quota constraint becomes non-binding, and the PCTSP algebraically and structurally reduces to the PTP, assuming costs are optimized against the lack of necessity to visit any node.20 Conversely, if ![][image26] is set to the sum of all available profits in the graph, the PCTSP forces a visit to every node, algebraically reducing to the classical TSP.6

### **The Orienteering Problem (OP) and Team Orienteering Problem (TOP)**

The Orienteering Problem (OP) is the single-vehicle variant where the objective is strictly the maximization of collected profit, but it is constrained by a strict upper bound on the travel cost or a strict time budget (![][image29]).2 The OP derives its name from the outdoor sport of orienteering, where competitors must visit various checkpoints to accumulate scores within a fixed time limit.5 The Team Orienteering Problem (TOP) is the ![][image1]\-hard multi-vehicle generalization of the OP, extending the model to a fleet of ![][image30] identical vehicles operating from the depot.10

The classical compact Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) formulation for the TOP utilizes an additional index ![][image4] to differentiate the vehicles in the fleet. The binary variable ![][image31] indicates if vehicle ![][image4] traverses the edge ![][image18].14 The objective is to maximize the total collected profit:

![][image32]
This objective is subject to several critical constraint sets:

1. **Node Visitation Constraint**: Ensures that a customer is visited at most once by the entire fleet: ![][image33].14
2. **Flow Conservation Constraint**: Ensures route continuity for each active vehicle: ![][image34].14
3. **Maximum Duration/Budget Constraint**: Enforces the strict time limit on every individual route: ![][image35].14
4. **Subtour Elimination Constraints (SECs)**: Prevents the formation of disconnected loops.14

Recent literature on exact models has advanced beyond the compact formulation by proposing pseudo-polynomial time-indexed formulations for the TOP. In this advanced structure, the graph is mathematically modified to include artificial intermediate vertices ![][image36] for each arc ![][image37], possessing a demand equal to the travel time of the original arc. Flow variables are then indexed by the exact departure time, ![][image38], completely eliminating the need for a specific vehicle index ![][image4].14 This formulation treats the TOP as a pure flow problem where traversing an arc inherently consumes the time resource, yielding significantly tighter linear programming relaxations and stronger lower bounds than the classical compact MILP.14

## **Exact Solvers and Advanced Decomposition Methodologies**

Solving VRPP variants to mathematically guaranteed optimality necessitates navigating highly fractured, non-convex polyhedra. Commercial general-purpose solvers, such as Gurobi or CPLEX, which rely on standard Branch-and-Bound algorithms, fail rapidly as the number of nodes ![][image39] scales. This failure is fundamentally due to weak linear programming (LP) relaxations and the combinatorial explosion of the search tree.2 To bridge the substantial optimality gaps, exact methodologies must rely heavily on deep polyhedral studies, specialized cutting planes, and structural decomposition techniques.10

### **Branch-and-Cut (B\&C) Algorithms**

Branch-and-Cut (B\&C) algorithms aggressively improve the LP relaxation bound at each node of the search tree by dynamically identifying and appending violated valid inequalities that cut off fractional, infeasible LP solutions without eliminating valid integer solutions.7 For single-vehicle problems like the PCTSP and PTP, a primary computational bottleneck is the exponential number of Subtour Elimination Constraints (SECs). Because the VRPP does not strictly require all nodes to be visited, classical SECs must be mathematically lifted into Generalized Subtour Elimination Constraints (GSECs).

Exact B\&C methods for the PCTSP leverage highly specialized heuristic separation procedures to efficiently identify violated primitive comb inequalities. Studies have proven that separation heuristics originally designed for the classical TSP can be mathematically adapted to separate primitive comb inequalities introduced specifically for the PCTSP, drastically reducing the integrality gap before the algorithm is forced to resort to spatial branching.10 In exact algorithms for the OP and TOP, B\&C architectures dynamically incorporate bounding mechanisms obtained from related optimization frameworks. For instance, bounding functions can be derived by solving an equivalent ![][image40] knapsack problem on the remaining node profits and weights to calculate the maximum theoretical profit of an unrouted segment, actively pruning branches of the search tree that cannot possibly exceed the incumbent best solution.10

### **Branch-and-Price (B\&P) and Column Generation**

For multi-vehicle routing scenarios, prominently the TOP and CPTP, Branch-and-Price (B\&P) stands as the preeminent exact methodology.10 B\&P applies Dantzig-Wolfe decomposition to reformulate the compact MILP into an extensive Set Partitioning or Set Packing model.14 In this reformulated master problem, each decision variable ![][image41] represents the selection of a feasible, complete route ![][image42] generated for a vehicle. The parameter ![][image43] defines the cost or profit of the route, and a binary coefficient matrix ![][image44] indicates whether customer ![][image21] is serviced by route ![][image42].14

Because enumerating the millions of feasible routes a priori is mathematically impossible, the algorithm employs Column Generation. The master problem is first solved as a linear relaxation using an artificially restricted set of initial routes (the Restricted Master Problem). The dual variables ![][image45] corresponding to the master problem's node-visitation constraints are extracted. These duals are then passed to a pricing subproblem. The mathematical objective of the pricing subproblem is to identify a new route ![][image42] with a strictly positive reduced cost (in a profit maximization context):

![][image46]
If such a route is found, it is added to the master problem as a new column, and the master problem is re-solved. The pricing subproblem in the TOP is modeled as an Elementary Shortest Path Problem with Resource Constraints (ESPPRC), which itself is strongly ![][image1]\-hard. State-of-the-art exact B\&P frameworks solve the ESPPRC using bidirectional dynamic programming labeling algorithms.10 The complexity of the labeling algorithm is ![][image47], where ![][image48] is the time budget, utilizing recursions such as ![][image49].14 To prevent combinatorial explosion in the subproblem, sophisticated mechanisms like decremental state-space relaxation (DSSR) and aggressive 2-cycle elimination dominance rules are deployed.10

When Column Generation converges at the root node but fails to close the integrality gap (yielding a fractional route selection), the algorithm must branch. State-of-the-art frameworks use a Branch-Cut-and-Price (BCP) schema. To avoid destroying the structure of the pricing subproblem, the algorithm branches on the original node visitation variables ![][image50] or edge variables ![][image51], rather than the route variables ![][image52].14 Families of valid inequalities are directly incorporated into the master problem during the process. Prominent cuts include Min Cut inequalities, which target fractional solutions where flow "leaks" in and out of a vertex multiple times, and Triangle Clique Cuts, which define compatibility graphs based on the impossibility of time-indexed arcs belonging to the same valid route.14

### **Benders Decomposition and Lagrangian Relaxation Formulations**

When the VRPP introduces severe non-linear dynamics into the cost or profit structures, standard B\&P architectures struggle to converge. A prime example is humanitarian search and rescue applications modeled as a TOP with time-varying profits. In such scenarios, the profit function is strictly nonconcave because survival rates decline rapidly over time; the profit collected depends entirely on the exact arrival time and service duration at the node.7

To solve this mathematically complex variant, researchers deploy Benders Decomposition, effectively separating the integer assignment of nodes to vehicles from the continuous scheduling variables that dictate the exact service times.7 A Benders Branch-and-Cut (BBC) algorithm iteratively solves a relaxed mixed-integer nonconcave master problem. When an integer assignment is found, the subproblem checks the feasibility of the time schedule. If infeasible or suboptimal regarding the nonconcave profit, Benders optimality and feasibility cuts are generated and injected into the master problem. This approach has proven capable of finding mathematically exact solutions for non-linear time-varying TOP instances where standard MINLP solvers, such as SCIP, fail completely to find an optimal bound.7

Similarly, Lagrangian Relaxation is extensively used to compute highly robust lower and upper bounds for the OP and TOP. By dualizing the complex maximum route duration constraints (or flow conservation constraints) directly into the objective function via a vector of Lagrangian multipliers ![][image53], the highly constrained routing problem is relaxed into a Minimum Directed 1-Subtree Problem (MD1SP) or a series of significantly easier unconstrained shortest path problems.10 Subgradient optimization algorithms are then utilized to iteratively refine the Lagrangian multipliers, penalizing constraint violations and driving the relaxed bound remarkably close to the true optimal integer solution.10

## **Matheuristics: The Hybridization of Mathematical Programming and Heuristics**

Despite the absolute mathematical elegance of BCP and Benders algorithms, exact solvers generally hit a severe scalability wall. Even with advanced subset-row inequalities and optimized dynamic programming, exact methods struggle to reliably solve VRPP graphs larger than 100 to 150 customer nodes within feasible runtimes.7 To address industrial-scale problems involving hundreds or thousands of nodes, Matheuristics have emerged as a dominant operational paradigm. Matheuristics actively exploit the rigorous mathematical programming formulations (MILP) of the VRPP but aggressively prune the search space and polyhedra using heuristic logic.30

A highly effective and widely adopted matheuristic framework for the VRPP utilizes the Set Partitioning formulation.30 Instead of solving the strongly ![][image1]\-hard ESPPRC to mathematical optimality at every node to generate columns, a metaheuristic—such as an Adaptive Large Neighborhood Search (ALNS) or an Iterated Local Search (ILS)—is deployed to rapidly explore the solution space. As the metaheuristic runs, it periodically dumps a massive pool of high-quality, feasible routes into a commercial LP solver. The solver then computes the mathematically optimal combination of these heuristically generated columns.30

Another sophisticated matheuristic approach is Kernel Search. In this methodology, the algorithm identifies a "kernel" of highly promising variables. This kernel typically consists of edges frequently used in high-quality heuristic solutions or edges identified as highly efficient via the Clarke-Wright savings method.30 The MILP solver is then restricted to only select edges from within this pre-defined kernel. By explicitly forcing the variables outside the kernel to zero, the dimension of the mathematical polyhedra is drastically reduced, allowing the exact solver to find mathematically superior solutions within the localized neighborhood in mere seconds.30

## **Metaheuristics for VRPP Variants**

For large-scale TOP, PTP, and PCTSP instances where mathematical optimality is not strictly required, metaheuristics remain the industry standard due to their computational speed, scalability, and acceptable optimality gaps.4 The core philosophical mechanism of metaheuristics applied to the VRPP requires the simultaneous optimization of node selection (knapsack inclusion) and route sequencing (TSP permutation).10

Single-trajectory search methods, notably Iterated Local Search (ILS) and Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS), are heavily utilized. In the TOP and its Time Window variants (TOPTW), ILS algorithms systematically utilize destruction and reconstruction phases to escape local optima.35 An ILS algorithm will remove a sub-chain of vertices from the incumbent route, and then re-insert nodes from the unvisited pool that maximize a specific profit-to-distance ratio heuristic.35 A known weakness of standard ILS is that it tends to overlook geographically distant but highly profitable node clusters. To counter this, state-of-the-art ILS implementations integrate sophisticated cluster-based extensions, grouping nodes geographically via K-means prior to the search, thereby making incursions into distant clusters mathematically attractive to the local search operator.38

Population-based methods—including Genetic Algorithms (GA), Ant Colony Optimization (ACO), and Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)—leverage multiple simultaneous search trajectories.30 In ACO, a population of artificial ants constructs routes by selecting subsequent nodes based on a probabilistic transition rule. This rule explicitly balances the immediate heuristic information (the profit-to-distance ratio of the next node) with the global pheromone trail deposited by previously successful ants on that specific edge.29

| Metaheuristic Class | Primary Algorithm | Key VRPP Mechanism | Application Strengths |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Single Trajectory** | Iterated Local Search (ILS) | Destruction/Reconstruction of route sub-chains. | Highly efficient for TOPTW; fast localized refinement.38 |
| **Single Trajectory** | Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS) | Systematic shaking across diverse topological neighborhoods. | Escaping deep local optima in unconstrained PTP.35 |
| **Population-Based** | Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) | Probabilistic edge selection based on global pheromone trails. | Excellent for routing under dynamic travel time uncertainty.31 |
| **Population-Based** | Genetic Algorithm (GA) | Crossover of route sequences; mutation via node swapping. | High exploration capability in highly scattered TOP profit topologies.30 |
| **Population-Based** | Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) | Velocity/Position updates mapped to discrete sequence permutations. | Continuous to discrete mapping; utilized in multi-vehicle CPTP.36 |

While metaheuristics offer massive speed improvements, their lack of strict theoretical guarantees requires rigorous empirical benchmarking. Evaluations on the standard TOP benchmark instances (such as those introduced by Chao et al. and Dang et al.) reveal that advanced metaheuristics generally achieve optimality gaps of between 0.5% and 5% compared to the best-known exact solutions.42 However, this performance degrades significantly on highly constrained, dynamically changing graphs or instances with exceedingly narrow time windows.43

## **Hyper-Heuristics and Automated Heuristic Design**

A major limitation of traditional metaheuristics is their deep domain dependency; an ILS algorithm perfectly tuned for the standard PCTSP often fails spectacularly when applied to the multi-vehicle CPTP without laborious, manual parameter tuning.47 Hyper-heuristics solve this limitation by elevating the search space entirely. Rather than searching over the space of *solutions*, a hyper-heuristic searches over the space of *heuristics*.39

In selection-based hyper-heuristics designed for the OP and TOP, the algorithm maintains a diverse pool of distinct low-level heuristics (LLHs) (e.g., greedy insertion, worst-removal, 2-opt, 3-opt, and SWAP operators).49 A high-level control strategy actively governs which LLH to apply at any given state of the optimization. Modern VRPP literature heavily relies on profit-based heuristic selection methods to drive this strategy. A prominent framework is the Sliding Multi-Armed Bandit (SlMAB) architecture, which models the selection of an LLH as a slot machine arm.49 The algorithm balances the exploitation of historically successful operators with the active exploration of untried LLHs using an Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) mathematical strategy, dynamically updating the expected reward of each heuristic based on its recent ability to improve the objective function.49

More advanced frameworks integrate true Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (MOEAs) into the high-level hyper-heuristic architecture. For instance, the MOHH-TOPTWR algorithm, explicitly designed for robotic search and rescue operations, uses Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithms (NSGA-III) and MOEA based on Decomposition (MOEA/D) to actively select and order LLHs.47 This allows the hyper-heuristic to simultaneously optimize competing objective functions, such as maximizing collected rescue profits, minimizing overall operational time, and managing non-linear battery depletion rates.47

Furthermore, Hyper-Heuristics based on Cumulative Reward Q-Learning (HHCRQL) reformulate the heuristic selection mechanism directly as a Markov Decision Process (MDP).53 A Q-learning agent uses a highly engineered four-dimensional state space to continually evaluate the topological progression of the route. The agent selects the LLH that maximizes a strict cumulative reward function that combines the immediate fitness improvement with the progression of the algorithmic timestep.53 In rigorous computational evaluations against state-of-the-art algorithms, HHCRQL demonstrated exceptional runtime stability and reduced optimality gaps by up to 9.17% against baseline Iterated Local Search and 6.74% against Large Neighborhood Search models on massive multi-trip VRP instances.53

## **Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO)**

The absolute frontier of routing optimization is Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO). NCO seeks to bypass iterative algorithmic search entirely by training deep neural networks to parameterize mathematically optimal routing policies directly from data.54 NCO frameworks applied to the VRPP primarily utilize sequence-to-sequence architectures, Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), and Transformer-based models to capture the underlying topological structure of the graph.54

The standard architecture relies on an Encoder-Decoder structure coupled with a sophisticated Attention Mechanism.54 The encoder reads the graph topology—processing the spatial coordinates, normalized profit values, time limits, and distance matrices into a high-dimensional continuous node embedding. The decoder builds the route autoregressively, using a Pointer Network or a Multi-Head Attention layer to output a continuous probability distribution over the available, unvisited nodes at each discrete step.54 For multi-vehicle extensions like the TOP, multi-agent transformer approaches (e.g., TOP-Former) are deployed. These architectures allow multiple decoding heads to construct routes for multiple vehicles simultaneously, implicitly learning the complex spatial coordination and demand splitting required to maximize global team profit without explicitly hard-coding the vehicle capacity constraints into the model.56

Because generating millions of exact, ground-truth optimal solutions is computationally impossible for supervised learning on large graphs, NCO models are overwhelmingly trained using Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL).54 Frameworks typically employ policy-gradient methods like the REINFORCE algorithm utilizing a simple deterministic greedy rollout as a baseline, or more advanced Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) architectures.54 Value-based deep learning methods, such as Deep Q-Networks (DQN), are frequently evaluated but generally exhibit severe degradation when attempting to generalize to larger problem sizes, as learning exact state-action values becomes mathematically intractable compared to learning a generalized policy distribution.55 The reward function for these models is structurally aligned with the specific VRPP variant; for the OP, the episodic reward is simply the sum of the profits of the visited nodes, with massive negative gradient penalties applied if the hard time constraint ![][image29] is violated during the rollout.54

Recent advances have also led to the development of Non-Autoregressive (NAR) models for NCO (e.g., NAR4TSP), which decode the entire routing sequence simultaneously rather than step-by-step, fundamentally breaking the conditional dependence chain to drastically increase inference speed.61 Furthermore, models like Sym-NCO explicitly exploit the inherent mathematical symmetry present in routing problems (where a route traversed backwards has an identical cost and profit profile).57 By injecting permutation invariance directly into the DRL training loop, Sym-NCO achieves exceptional convergence, resulting in a documented 240x speedup over conventional Iterated Local Search methods on complex PCTSP benchmarks.63

However, deep NCO methods face severe fundamental limitations regarding generalization. An NCO model trained exclusively on 50-node TOP instances typically collapses mathematically when evaluated on 200-node instances, as the input graph falls entirely out-of-distribution regarding the learned embeddings.55 Furthermore, enforcing strict feasibility constraints (such as absolute quota requirements in PCTSP or hard time windows in TOPTW) remains a severe structural challenge for neural architectures, as the continuous probability space of softmax outputs inherently struggles to strictly partition feasible from infeasible sub-spaces.55

## **Non-Neural Machine Learning for Feature and Graph Reduction**

Due to the deep generalization limitations, lack of mathematical guarantees, and the immense hardware training overhead of Deep Neural Networks, there is a powerful resurgence in utilizing "non-neural" classical Machine Learning algorithms to solve the VRPP. These methodologies offer vastly enhanced interpretability, strictly lower computational complexity, and independence from perfectly balanced training datasets.66

Classical supervised and unsupervised learning algorithms—such as Random Forests, Support Vector Machines (SVM), Generalized Linear Models, and K-Means clustering—are aggressively utilized to mathematically manipulate and reduce the search space before any routing algorithm is applied.4

In the VRPP, the most computationally devastating challenge is the initial knapsack problem: mathematically determining which specific subset of nodes should be visited out of ![][image3] possibilities.9 Random Forest regression models and SVMs utilizing non-convex penalties are highly effective when trained to predict "customer selection likelihoods" or "edge traversal probabilities".69 By analyzing complex spatial features, historical profit densities, demand fluctuations, and depot proximity, an SVM can precisely quantify the probability that a specific customer node ![][image21] belongs to the optimal solution graph.69 Nodes or edges with highly negative predictive likelihoods are immediately pruned from the active graph. This dimensionality reduction is mathematically profound. Reducing a 200-node TOP graph to a 100-node graph prior to optimization drops the fundamental complexity of the subset selection space from ![][image54] to ![][image55], allowing exact Branch-and-Price solvers to successfully process instances that were previously mathematically intractable.69

Similarly, K-Means clustering is utilized as a vital a priori topological transformation. Nodes are partitioned into distinct spatial clusters based on multidimensional feature vectors that include geographic coordinates and relative profit margins.4 A routing algorithm (like the Clarke-Wright savings method or an exact BCP formulation) is then run exclusively intra-cluster, followed by a secondary inter-cluster connection optimization. This directly reduces the dense, complete graph ![][image56] into a highly sparse adjacency matrix, massively accelerating the pricing subproblem in Column Generation architectures.4

## **Tabular Reinforcement Learning and Linear Function Approximation**

In direct parallel with the resurgence of non-neural ML, classical Tabular Reinforcement Learning algorithms—specifically Q-Learning, SARSA (State-Action-Reward-State-Action), and Double Q-Learning—provide highly effective routing policies for the VRPP without relying on the opaque, computationally heavy deep function approximators required by NCO.75

In Tabular Q-Learning implementations for the OP, the agent maintains an explicit, mathematically discrete ![][image57]\-table. This table represents the exact expected sum of future profits for taking a specific action (visiting unvisited node ![][image58]) from a specific state (being located at node ![][image21] with a specific discrete amount of time budget remaining).75 The mathematical formulation of the reward signal is critical to the convergence of the Q-table. Literature demonstrates various reward engineering techniques to balance distance and profit, such as ![][image59], ![][image60], and ![][image61], ensuring the agent is appropriately penalized for selecting geographically distant nodes even if they possess high raw profits.77

Because the true state space of a VRPP is continuous regarding elapsed time and spatial coordinates, standard Tabular RL suffers from the curse of dimensionality, requiring infinitely large Q-tables. To solve this mathematically, researchers employ rigorous state aggregation or Linear Function Approximation.81 In RL with Linear Function Approximation, the value of a state is represented not in a table, but as the inner product of a dynamically learned weight vector and a set of predefined, handcrafted basis features (e.g., geometric distance to the nearest unvisited high-profit node, exact remaining time budget, global vehicle capacity).81 Unlike Deep RL, RL with linear function approximation applied in Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) that satisfy strict linear Bellman completeness provides algorithms that are not only statistically tractable but provably convergent to optimal policies under mild assumptions.81

Non-neural RL architectures demonstrate massive inference speed advantages that are critical for industrial application. Studies directly comparing tabular RL (enhanced with greedy heuristics) against standard deep neural architectures for time-window constrained routing show that the non-neural inference process is 100 to 1,000 times faster.65 This makes Linear Function Approximation RL exceptionally viable for real-time, highly dynamic fleet re-routing where sub-second computational latency is strictly mandated by the operational environment.65 Furthermore, Tabular RL is frequently integrated directly *into* classical exact methods or metaheuristics as a sub-routine; for example, Q-learning algorithms are aggressively used to dynamically adjust the temperature decay parameters of Simulated Annealing, or to construct the initial backbone graph in online, real-time vehicle routing frameworks.52

## **Computational Benchmarking and Operational Scalability**

The profound theoretical rigor of these diverse mathematical approaches is ultimately validated solely through rigorous computational benchmarking. The academic standard for evaluating the TOP relies on heavily documented benchmark instance sets developed fundamentally by Chao et al. (comprising 157 instances ranging from 64 to 102 nodes) and Dang et al. (comprising 82 highly complex instances ranging from 102 to 401 nodes).22

Recent benchmarking of commercial mathematical optimization solvers illustrates the strict, hard mathematical limitations of pure MILP approaches on these benchmark sets when the algorithms are constrained by realistic operational runtimes (e.g., a strict 1-minute time limit on standard multi-core CPUs).

| Instance Set Origin | Node Count | Hexaly Gap (%) | OR-Tools Gap (%) | Gurobi Gap (%) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Chao et al.** | 64-66 | 0.0 | 2.3 | 3.8 |
| **Chao et al.** | 100-102 | 0.3 | 9.5 | 5.1 |
| **Dang et al.** | 100-200 | 0.3 | 11.7 | 22.7 |
| **Dang et al.** | 200-300 | 0.8 | 13.0 | 63.5 |
| **Dang et al.** | 300-400 | 1.3 | 15.5 | 90.9 |

Data sourced from comprehensive computational benchmarking analysis utilizing Hexaly 14.5, Google OR-Tools 9.14, and Gurobi 11.0, strictly constrained to a 1-minute execution time.22

The performance data encapsulated in the benchmarking table clearly defines the absolute operational boundary of classical MILP solvers when applied to the VRPP. Gurobi, universally recognized as representing the apex of exact Branch-and-Cut MILP solvers, successfully manages small instances with near optimality. However, it experiences catastrophic mathematical degradation on the large-scale Dang instances, yielding an unacceptable 90.9% optimality gap at the 300-400 node threshold.22 This degradation occurs fundamentally because the time-indexed or compact formulations create massive, exceptionally weak linear programming relaxations that the branch-and-bound tree simply cannot prune effectively within the strict time limits mandated by real-world operations. The sheer volume of fractional variables overwhelms the cutting plane generation.22

Google OR-Tools, which relies heavily on constraint programming and guided local search mechanisms rather than pure MILP relaxations, scales noticeably smoother but still plateaus at a highly suboptimal 15.5% gap for large graphs.22 Conversely, Hexaly, operating as a specialized global optimization solver built entirely on nonlinear and set-based modeling variables, leverages highly optimized Local Search and built-in Column Generation natively under the hood. Consequently, it maintains an impressive optimality gap of less than 2% across all instance sizes, successfully finding solutions with gaps lower than 5% for every single instance evaluated within the stringent 1-minute limit.22

This benchmarking reality underscores precisely why matheuristics, machine-learning-reduced polyhedra, and reinforcement-learning-guided hyper-heuristics have utterly consumed modern operations research. While exact Branch-Cut-and-Price algorithms guarantee mathematical truth and absolute optimality, their exponential time complexity renders them virtually useless for real-time, dynamic logistics applications. The operational domain strictly demands architectural frameworks that can seamlessly blend the robust mathematical bounding of exact solvers with the infinitely scalable traversal mechanisms of machine learning and heuristic search.

## **Conclusion**

The Vehicle Routing Problem with Profits represents one of the most theoretically challenging and practically significant intersections of continuous time-scheduling, discrete assignment, and combinatorial sequencing in the entirety of modern Operations Research. The fundamental transition from classical cost-minimization logic to profit-maximization radically transforms the underlying mathematical polyhedra, breaking traditional solver architectures and demanding entirely new classes of highly specialized algorithms.

Exact methods, particularly advanced Branch-Cut-and-Price algorithms fortified with specialized valid inequalities and Benders Decomposition for nonconcave objective functions, remain absolutely essential for determining true lower and upper bounds. Yet, they consistently encounter insurmountable scalability walls beyond modest graph sizes due to combinatorial explosion. To directly address this fundamental mathematical limitation, the field has successfully and permanently shifted toward hybrid algorithmic architectures. Matheuristics now efficiently leverage the rigorous mathematical formulations of exact solvers while strictly constraining the searchable polyhedra via metaheuristic node exploration and kernel identification.

Simultaneously, the aggressive infusion of Artificial Intelligence has irrevocably altered the trajectory of VRPP research. While deep Neural Combinatorial Optimization offers the profound promise of end-to-end, instantaneous policy learning via Transformer architectures, it is currently severely hindered by crippling issues of generalizability, dataset bias, and hard constraint satisfaction. Instead, the most computationally powerful, industrially viable, and rigorously verifiable advances are occurring directly at the intersection of non-neural Machine Learning, Tabular Reinforcement Learning, and traditional mathematical optimization. By deploying Random Forests and Support Vector Machines to mathematically predict edge likelihoods and prune graph dimensions, utilizing tabular Q-Learning to dictate dynamic hyper-heuristic operator selection, and applying linear function approximations to achieve real-time, mathematically convergent re-routing, researchers are consistently achieving near-optimal solutions at fractions of the latency required by classical solvers. The synthesis of learning-based dimensionality reduction with exact mathematical bounding clearly stands as the definitive, undeniable framework for the next generation of advanced logistics optimization.

Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Agent Stochastic Resource Collection \- ResearchGate, accessed April 27, 2026, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/369311843\_Reinforcement\_Learning\_for\_Multi-Agent\_Stochastic\_Resource\_Collection](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/369311843_Reinforcement_Learning_for_Multi-Agent_Stochastic_Resource_Collection)
84. A Reinforcement Learning Framework for Efficient Informative Sensing \- ResearchGate, accessed April 27, 2026, [https://www.researchgate.net/publication/347190860\_A\_Reinforcement\_Learning\_Framework\_for\_Efficient\_Informative\_Sensing](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/347190860_A_Reinforcement_Learning_Framework_for_Efficient_Informative_Sensing)
85. A Learning—Based Particle Swarm Optimizer for Solving Mathematical Combinatorial Problems \- MDPI, accessed April 27, 2026, [https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1680/12/7/643](https://www.mdpi.com/2075-1680/12/7/643)
86. Online Large-Scale Taxi Assignment: Optimization and Learning | Published in Findings, accessed April 27, 2026, [https://findingspress.org/article/74765-online-large-scale-taxi-assignment-optimization-and-learning](https://findingspress.org/article/74765-online-large-scale-taxi-assignment-optimization-and-learning)
